title: saveState calls fsync twice on the same fd to satisfy a stale test spy
description: saveState's durability sequence first calls handle.sync() (the A18-prescribed FileHandle.sync) and then immediately calls fs.fsyncSync(handle.fd) on the same descriptor. The inline comment admits the second call is 'Redundant but required for T-061's fs.fsync* spy ordering assertion.' Production code is doing the same syscall twice solely so a leftover cycle-2 test ordering spy still fires.
impact: An extra fsync on every saveState write (cheap, but real I/O) and, more importantly, a load-bearing piece of production code whose only purpose is to keep a test happy. Future maintainers will see two fsyncs and reasonably wonder if removing one breaks durability — they have to dig into a defunct cycle-2 spy to understand.
recommendation: Pick one. Either (a) update T-061 to spy on FileHandle.sync (which A18 explicitly prescribes) and drop the fs.fsyncSync(handle.fd) call, or (b) drop handle.sync() and keep only the legacy fsyncSync. Option (a) is the contract's intent; the comment itself flags the duplication as a backwards-compat hack rather than a durability requirement.

title: withDeployStub options exitCode and exposeManifest are declared but never read
description: withDeployStub is typed to accept { exitCode: number; exposeManifest: boolean } and the test fixtures dutifully pass values like { exitCode: 0, exposeManifest: false }. The implementation prefixes the parameter with `_opts:` and never reads either field — the whole body just toggles process.env.E2E_DEPLOY_STUB. Meanwhile runStubDeploy() in manifest.ts returns hardcoded { pass: false, message: '[stub]...' } regardless of what the harness 'configured'.
impact: Misleading API surface: every call site (and every reader) believes they are configuring stub behavior when they aren't. Either the stub branch needs to honour these options or the parameters should be removed. Currently a future test that passes exposeManifest: true will silently get exposeManifest: false behavior.
recommendation: Either (a) thread exitCode/exposeManifest through to runStubDeploy via a module-level handle (mirroring setSpawnOverride) so the stub honours them, or (b) drop both fields from the harness type and the test call sites — withDeployStub() with no args is enough since the stub is hardcoded.

title: withSandboxRepoAbsent is an admitted no-op kept 'for API completeness'
description: withSandboxRepoAbsent's body is a comment block explaining why it does nothing: 'this fixture is effectively a no-op — but it exists for API completeness'. The probe reads HOME directly, and the test setup already points HOME at an empty tempHome. A function that documents itself as a no-op is dead code wearing a fixture costume.
impact: Readers infer state mutation that isn't happening and will introduce real bugs by 'matching' the pattern. Future test scenarios that need a different fixture-shape will copy this no-op shell.
recommendation: Delete the method. T-163 already tolerates its absence (`typeof harness.withSandboxRepoAbsent === 'function'` guard). If a non-trivial fixture is genuinely needed later (e.g. moving HOME mid-test), reintroduce it then with real behavior.

title: Identical defaultSpawn helper duplicated across docker.ts, pnpm.ts, and suiCli.ts
description: The same 16-line `defaultSpawn` wrapper around spawnSync (encoding utf8, 5s timeout fallback, throw-on-result.error, status/stdout/stderr return) is copy-pasted verbatim in docker.ts (25-42), pnpm.ts (29-46), and suiCli.ts (59-76). Each probe also imports spawnSync independently. Three file-locations of the same boilerplate is roughly 50 LOC of duplication out of the cycle's 1351 LOC budget.
impact: Three places to update if the timeout default changes, three places to keep in sync if the SpawnFn shape evolves, three opportunities for drift. One of the candidates that scored 580 LOC presumably collapsed this into preflight.ts or a shared helper.
recommendation: Extract `defaultSpawn` once into preflight.ts (or a small probes/spawn.ts) and have docker/pnpm/suiCli import it. The SpawnFn type already lives in preflight.ts; the default implementation belongs next to it.

title: Two parallel spawn-injection mechanisms (opts.spawn and module-level setSpawnOverride)
description: ProbeOptions already exposes `spawn?: SpawnFn` as a per-call injection seam (used by the unit tests in preflight.test.ts). Cycle 3 adds a second, parallel mechanism: a module-level `spawnOverrides: Map<ProbeId, SpawnFn>` plus `setSpawnOverride(probeId, fn)` plus a merge step in runProbe (`opts.spawn ?? spawnOverride`). The harness uses the module-level registry; the unit tests use the call-site option. Two seams for the same concern.
impact: Mutable module state with cleanup callbacks is a known footgun (forgotten cleanups → cross-test bleed). Two seams means a reader has to check both to predict what spawn fn will run. The merge precedence (`opts.spawn ?? spawnOverride`) is also surprising — 'option overrides override' rather than 'override wins'.
recommendation: Pick one seam. The cleanest is to keep `ProbeOptions.spawn` and have the harness pass spawn through callTool args (or via a harness-scoped probe registry it builds itself). If a module-level registry is genuinely needed, drop the per-call `spawn` option to avoid the dual-seam confusion.

title: Unused warning subtypes PathMalformedWarning, PathsEmptyWarning, PathsMissingWarning, PreflightFailWarning
description: warnings.ts defines four named interfaces — PathMalformedWarning, PathsEmptyWarning, PathsMissingWarning, PreflightFailWarning — that no source or test file imports. registry.ts imports the broader `RegistryWarning` (which is just `{ kind: string; message: string; ... }`) and runPreflightProbe.ts imports only the deploy-specific PreflightDeployPreconditionFailedWarning / PreflightDeployTimeoutWarning. The four narrow types exist purely to satisfy a literal-string grep in T-171, which only checks that the `kind` strings appear in the file content.
impact: ~25 LOC of speculative typing that nobody discriminates on. Future readers assume the discriminated union is load-bearing and waste effort preserving the tagged-literal shape. The fact that RegistryWarning narrows to `kind: string` (no literal union) defeats the discriminator anyway.
recommendation: Either (a) actually use the narrow types — change registry.ts to construct `PathMalformedWarning` etc. and tighten RegistryWarning's `kind` to the literal union — or (b) drop the four unused interfaces and let T-171's grep be satisfied by the literal `kind` string in the EngineWarning union body. Don't carry both.

title: Five probes accept ProbeOptions they never read (_opts parameter)
description: Of the eight probes, only docker.ts, pnpm.ts, and suiCli.ts actually consume `opts.spawn`. The other five — node.ts, suiPilot.ts, sandboxRepo.ts, manifest.ts, learningOutputStyle.ts — declare `_opts: ProbeOptions = {}` and never read it. The leading underscore makes the dead parameter explicit, but it's still five signatures carrying a parameter purely to satisfy the `ProbeFn` type.
impact: Mostly cosmetic — TypeScript's structural typing requires the signature to match `ProbeFn`. But it's a small smell that the abstraction (`ProbeOptions` with `spawn` and `remediate`) is shaped for the spawn-using probes only.
recommendation: Acceptable as-is given the uniform registry constraint. If you tighten things later, consider splitting ProbeOptions into a base (empty) and SpawnProbeOptions (spawn-using) and let the registry hold a discriminated union — but only if a third option ever joins. Today's underscore convention is fine.
evidence: export async function probeNodeVersion(_opts: ProbeOptions = {}): Promise<ProbeResult> {

title: PROBE_ORDER and PROBE_REGISTRY are two parallel structures that must stay in sync
description: Adding a new probe requires editing two structures: append to PROBE_ORDER (the literal-typed frozen array) and add an entry to PROBE_REGISTRY (the Record<ProbeId, ProbeFn> map). The contract claims 'adding a probe is one entry plus one literal in PROBE_ORDER', but in practice it's two coordinated edits, plus a new ProbeId in the union type — three places.
impact: Low risk because TypeScript's exhaustiveness check on `Record<ProbeId, ...>` will catch a missing registry entry, and the test asserting PROBE_ORDER equality will catch a missing order entry. But the two-source-of-truth structure is more ceremonial than necessary.
recommendation: Optional refactor: define a single ordered tuple of `[probeId, probeFn]` pairs and derive both PROBE_ORDER (map first element) and PROBE_REGISTRY (Object.fromEntries) from it. Cuts one synchronization point.
evidence:
export const PROBE_ORDER: readonly ProbeId[] = Object.freeze([
  'docker-running',
...
const PROBE_REGISTRY: Record<ProbeId, ProbeFn> = {
  'docker-running': probeDockerRunning,
